title: Engrosser's script — American Copperplate
materials: ruling pen or pencil for guide-lines at 52-54 degree slant
sources: Engrosser's Script is a 19th-century American development from English Roundhand Copperplate, codified in Michael Sull, The Art of Cursive Penmanship (1989, copyright, structural description only). Historical lineage from Edward Cocker's penmanship copybooks (17th century) through Platt Rogers Spencer — Spencerian Key to Practical Penmanship (1866) — public domain.
notes: Engrosser's Script is the highly formal American pointed-pen hand used for certificates, diplomas, and legal documents — the hand that 'engrosses' official papers. More upright than Spencerian (slope 52-54°), extremely thick-thin contrast, the overturn and underturn swells are pronounced. Distinguish from Spencerian: Engrosser's is more upright, more formal, wider thick strokes. Covers: the compound curve as the building block (entrance curve, body oval, exit shade), the capital alphabet (which relies on compound curves and flourished loops), consistency of the 52-54° slant across the page, oblique pen holder adjustment.
